description: Fix the critical first-run UX bug where opentabs start prints MCP client configs without the Authorization Bearer header, causing users to copy broken configs that fail with 401 errors.

userStories.title: Generate auth secret before printing MCP client configs on first run
userStories.description: As a new user running opentabs start for the first time, I want the printed MCP client configs to include the correct Authorization: Bearer header so that I can copy-paste them directly into my MCP client and have them work.
acceptanceCriteria: On first run (no ~/.opentabs/ directory exists), opentabs start prints MCP client configs that include 'Authorization: Bearer <secret>' in the headers field
The secret printed matches the one written to ~/.opentabs/extension/auth.json
On subsequent runs, the behavior is unchanged (secret is still included in the printed configs)
The auth.json file is still created at ~/.opentabs/extension/auth.json with correct 0600 permissions
userStories.notes:
Root cause: In platform/cli/src/commands/start.ts, handleStart() calls autoInitialize() (line 234) which installs the extension but does NOT generate auth.json. Then readAuthSecret() (line 235) reads auth.json which doesn't exist yet — returns null. The secret is only generated later by the MCP server process in platform/mcp-server/src/config.ts loadSecret() (lines 383-411).

Fix approach: Generate the auth secret in the CLI's first-run setup (autoInitialize or a new step) BEFORE readAuthSecret() is called. The secret generation logic already exists in the MCP server's loadSecret() — either extract the generation into a shared utility, or duplicate the simple crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') call in the CLI's setup code. The key is: write auth.json BEFORE the CLI reads it to print configs.

The generateSecret and writeAuthJson logic is in platform/mcp-server/src/config.ts. The CLI setup logic is in platform/cli/src/commands/setup.ts (installExtension function, lines 46-103). The setup.ts file currently creates the extension directory but not auth.json.

Important: The MCP server's loadSecret() must still work — it should detect that auth.json already exists and use the existing secret instead of regenerating. Check that loadSecret() has this 'if exists, read; else generate' logic.
